title-group.article-title: Metabolomic profiling of Bacillus amyloliquefaciens MU-10 and its antagonistic effects on Cercospora arachidicola

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Early leaf spot disease in peanut severely impacts crop yield and quality. In response, biological control by Bacillus strains offers an alternative approach to replace harmful chemical fungicides, as these bacteria produce bioactive secondary metabolites. The effectiveness of these metabolites greatly relies on the submerged fermentation process, which in turn depends on optimizing the nutrient media—especially since there is a significant difference between nutrient-optimized fermentation medium (OP) and nonoptimized fermentation medium (NOP).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: In the present study, we isolated an antagonistic strain, Bacillus amyloliquefaciens MU-10, from soil and identified it. According to in vitro experiments, strain MU-10 exhibited potent antifungal activity against C. arachidicola, with an inhibition zone of 76 mm. Similarly, under greenhouse conditions, the disease control efficiency of the MU-10 strain against peanut leaf spot disease was 93.33%. SEM analysis revealed that the hyphae of the pathogen were destroyed and that the effect of MU-10 reduced the abundance of spores. UHPLC-MS/MS was used to elucidate the bioactive secondary metabolites produced by strain MU-10 in OP and NOP submerged fermentation. A total of 831 metabolites were detected in OP, 32% of which were classified as organic acids. Among these were 83 differentially expressed metabolites (DEMs). According to the VIP scores, 12 metabolites were identified as key contributors to group separation, including the upregulated 3-O-acetylisopimaric acid and vanillylamine. Conversely, pyrrole-2-carboxylic acid was identified as a downregulated VIP metabolite in OP. The receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves confirmed all the metabolic results. Pure compound validation was confirmed by the use of commercially synthesized vanillylamine, pyrrole-2-carboxylic acid, and syringic acid as key antifungal agents.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Nutrient-optimized fermentation significantly increased the production of Bacillus-based biocontrol products. This improvement represents a significant advancement in sustainable, environmentally friendly crop disease management, providing an effective solution for controlling early leaf spot in peanuts.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s12866-025-04695-w.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Fungal diseases negatively impact crop yields and pose a significant risk to global food security [1, 2]. In peanut crops, early leaf spot disease caused by the fungus C. arachidicola significantly affects both crop yield and quality [3, 4]. These fungal diseases are mostly managed with chemical pesticides [5, 6]. However, excessive use of chemical fungicides induces severe and diverse resistance among pathogenic microorganisms [7, 8]. Furthermore, pesticide residues also impact ecosystems and biodiversity. This can even lead to environmental contamination and pose a threat to human health [9]. In light of these concerns, biological control is an environmentally friendly approach. Recently, this approach has gained significant attention [6, 10]. Research has shown that among the biocontrol agents, rhizobacteria have great potential as biocontrol agents [11]. In particular, Bacillus species play crucial roles in combating plant diseases and promoting plant growth [12, 13], especially B. amyloliquefaciens, which stands out as a significant antagonistic agent [14–17].

Plant disease management is accomplished primarily by the application of diverse antimicrobial compounds produced by these bacteria [18]. A whole-genome study of B. amyloliquefaciens has also revealed its complex mechanisms and exceptional capacity to produce secondary metabolites, increasing its biological potential. In recent research, the draft genome of the B. amyloliquefaciens strain Fito_F321 was used to predict bacillaene, difficidin, macrolactin, surfactin, and fengycin [19]. Similarly, genomic data mining of the B. amyloliquefaciens strain GD4a revealed that it has genes that produce metabolites such as indole 3-acetic acid, bacteriocin, surfactin, and fengycin [20]. The main bioactive compounds predicted to be synthesized by B. amyloliquefaciens are antifungal peptides, and these antifungal peptides are synthesized through ribosome and nonribosome mechanisms. As more genomes of various B. amyloliquefaciens strains are sequenced, significant variations have been observed in the gene clusters responsible for secondary metabolite production among these strains [21], and the active substances of some strains may be completely different, such as fengycin, iturin, surfactin [22], difficidin, bacilysin [23], and macrolactin [24], which are among the main antifungal peptides synthesized via the nonribosomal pathway of B. amyloliquefaciens.

The efficient microbial synthesis of secondary metabolites is precisely reliant upon the specific composition of the growth medium [25]. Microbial synthesis requires optimized fermentation conditions to increase productivity due to its complexity. The physical conditions and nutritional culture medium composition strongly affect microorganism fermentation dynamics [26]. In particular, the composition of the fermentation medium substantially affects both the yield and metabolic profile of microorganisms [27]. Secondary metabolites can also impact bacterial growth and influence metabolic rates during fermentation [28]. Comparing metabolic changes in these metabolites helps in building metabolic networks and in speculating on their functions [29]. Metabolomics is a valuable strategy for comprehensively profiling and analyzing metabolites in complex systems or fermentation processes [30].

The aim of the current work was to investigate the biocontrol potential of a newly isolated B. amyloliquefaciens MU-10 strain against the peanut leaf spot pathogen C. arachidicola and to employ liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (LC‒MS) and ultrahigh-performance liquid chromatography (UHPLC) metabolomics to determine how strainMU-10 production contributes to peanut leaf spot disease suppression.

sec.title: Microorganisms and growth conditions
sec.p: The pathogenic fungal strain C. arachidicola was obtained from the Institute of Plant Protection, Liaoning Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Shenyang. The antagonistic bacterial strain was isolated from cultivated peanut soil. A total of 10 soil samples were collected in sterile polythene bags, each containing 5 g of soil. A serial dilution was made to obtain a pure culture of the strain. The soil was added to distilled water and then transferred to LB agar medium or nutrient agar medium. The strain was incubated for 7 days at 28 °C, after which a colony was isolated and purified. The purified strain was screened against pathogenic C. arachidicola, and the antagonistic strain was selected on the basis of its strong antifungal activity. The pure antagonistic strain MU-10 was cultivated for 3 days at 28 °C in the dark on LB agar medium. Pathogenic C. arachidicola fungi were cultured for 14 days at 28 °C in the dark on potato dextrose agar (PDA). An inoculum suspension of C. arachidicola was inoculated with the pathogen in potato dextrose broth.

sec.title: Molecular identification of strains
sec.p: According to the manufacturer’s instructions, DNA was isolated from strain MU-10 via a DNA isolation kit (Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd.). A polymerase chain reaction was used to confirm the isolated bacterial strain (94°C for 10 minutes, followed by 34 cycles at 94°C for 30 s, 56°C for 30 s, and 72°C for 90 s, with a final extension at 72°C for 10 minutes). We amplified 16S rDNA sequences via the following forward and reverse primers: “(5’-ATGCCATTCGTGCGGAGGTTG-3′), (5’-CGTCTCTGCTGTCATCACTTCGTAT-3)’’. The gyrA gene sequence was amplified via the specific primers gyrA-F and gyrA-R ‘‘(CAGTCAGGAAATGCGTACGTCCTT, CAAGGTAATGCTCCAGGCATTGCT)’’.

PCR amplification was carried out under the following cycling conditions: initial denaturation at 94 °C for 2 min, followed by 35 cycles of denaturation at 94 °C for 30 s, annealing at 50 °C for 30 s, and extension at 72 °C for 1 min, with a final extension at 72 °C for 1 min. The obtained sequences were compared against the National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI) BLAST database (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov). Sequences showing high similarity (> 95%) to the target sequence were selected and downloaded. Multiple sequence alignment was performed via MEGA version 7.0. Phylogenetic relationships were reconstructed via Bayesian inference implemented in PhyloSuite v1.2.2 [31]. Bayesian phylogenetic analyses were conducted in MrBayes v3.2.6 [32] under a partitioned model, with two independent runs of 2,000,000 generations; the first 25% of the sampled trees were discarded as burn-in. The resulting trees were visualized and edited via FigTree v1.4.4 (http://tree.bio.ed.ac.uk/software/figtree). The nucleotide sequence was deposited in GenBank under the accession number PP922374.

sec.title: Morphological, physiological, and biochemical identification
sec.p: Strain MU-10 was cultivated under defined conditions for morphological analysis. The samples were fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde and dehydrated in a graded ethanol series (30%, 50%, 70%, 90%, and 100%) to remove water and prevent structural collapse. The dehydrated samples were coated with gold. The morphology of strain MU-10 was examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi, Japan) [33]. Strain MU-10 was also streaked onto LB media and incubated at 27 °C for 24 h. Colony morphology was assessed, and physiological and biochemical characteristics were identified following standard protocols described by Zang et al.2023 [34].

sec.title: Non optimized fermentation
sec.p: A two-step submerged fermentation procedure was performed. First, a spore suspension was synthesized by inoculating 40 mL of Luria–Bertani (LB) medium with two 5-mm MU-10 spore cakes. The fermentation medium was then prepared with 7 g/L yeast extract, 10 g/L tryptone, and 2.5 g/L NaCl, and the pH was adjusted to between 6.6 and 6.8. After autoclave sterilization, 5 mL of spore suspension (1 × 109 CFU/mL) was added to each flask of sterilized nutrient medium. After inoculation, the flasks were incubated in a rotary shaker at 30 °C and 165 rpm for 96 h.

sec.title: Optimized fermentation
sec.p: The same two-step submerged fermentation procedure described above was executed. In this part of the experiment, we used the optimized fermentation medium determined in our prior research for cultivating the B. amyloliquefaciens strain, as described by Ahsan et al.,2022 [35]. These optimized nutrients were selected on the basis of their ability to enhance antifungal bioactivity. The composition of the OP medium was as follows: 15 g/L semolina flour, 12.5 g/L beef extract, and 0.5 g/L MgSO4. The pH was carefully adjusted to 6.6–6.8.

sec.title: In vitro bioassays of the effects of strain MU-10 against C. arachidicola
sec.p: We assessed the inhibition of C. arachidicola by strain MU-10 via the Oxford cup method. The treatments included OP and NOP fermentation broths. We filled a 90-mm petri plate with 18 mL of melted PDA and then inoculated it with 5 mL of C. arachidicola spore suspension (10 × 7 spores/mL). An Oxford cup was placed at the center, and 200 µL of fermentation broth was injected via an Eppendorf pipette. The control group received the fungicide tebuconazole at the same treatment dose as the other groups did. The Ck group, as a negative control, was inoculated with only C. arachidicola. The plates were incubated at 28 °C without light for 14 days. We measured inhibition zones to evaluate the potential of MU-10 against C. arachidicola. All the trials were performed in triplicate.

sec.title: Action of the Mu-10 strain against C. arachidicola mycelia and spores
sec.p: Morphological changes were investigated via SEM (Hitachi, Japan). To investigate the impact of the MU-10 strain on C. arachidicola, we selected hyphae that were in close proximity to the border of the antifungal zone.

sec.title: Disease control effects in greenhouse
Extraction of metabolites
sec.p: A volume of 100 µL of the sample was gently added to a 2 ml EP tube. Next, 400 µL of the extract mixture, consisting of a mixture of methanol and acetonitrile at a 1:1 ratio, along with an isotopically labeled internal standard mixture, was added. The samples were subjected to homogenization at a frequency of 35 Hz for 4 min, followed by sonication for 5 min in an ice‒water bath. The processes of homogenization and sonication were iterated three times. The samples were incubated for 1 h at -40 °C and subsequently subjected to centrifugation at 12,000 rpm (RCF = 13800×g, R = 8.6 cm) via a centrifuge rotor with a radius of 8.6 cm. This centrifugation process lasted for 15 min at a temperature of 4 °C. The resulting supernatant was carefully transferred to a pristine glass vial in preparation for subsequent analysis. The QC sample was prepared by combining equimolar volumes of the supernatants obtained from each sample.

sec.title: LC‒MS/MS analysis
sec.p: LC‒MS/MS analyses were conducted via an ultrahigh-performance liquid chromatography (UHPLC) system, specifically the Vanquish model from Thermo Fisher Scientific. Chromatographic separation was achieved via a Waters BEH amide column with dimensions of 2.1 mm × 50 mm and a particle size of 1.7 μm. This column was coupled to an Orbitrap Exploris 120 mass spectrometer (Orbitrap MS) manufactured by Thermo. For the mobile phase, a solution consisting of 25 mmol/L ammonium acetate and 25 mmol/L ammonia hydroxide in water was prepared, resulting in a pH of 9.75 (referred to as solution A). Additionally, acetonitrile (referred to as solution B) was used as a component of the mobile phase.

The autosampler was maintained at a temperature of 4 °C, and the injection volume was precisely 2 µL. The Orbitrap Exploris 120 mass spectrometer was used because of its ability to obtain MS/MS spectra through the implementation of information-dependent acquisition (IDA) mode within the confines of the acquisition software (Xcalibur, Thermo). In this mode, the acquisition software perpetually assesses the comprehensive scan mass spectrum. The ESI source conditions were configured as follows: the sheath gas flow rate was set to 50 Arb, the auxiliary gas flow rate was set to 15Arb, the capillary temperature was maintained at 350 °C, the full MS resolution was set to 60,000, the MS/MS resolution was set to 30,000, and the collision energy was set to 20/30/40 in NCE mode. The spray voltage was set to 3 kV for positive ionization or -3 kV for negative ionization.

sec.title: Analysis of differential metabolites and metabolic pathways
sec.p: In this investigation, a total of four quality control (QC) samples and twelve experimental samples were utilized. A remarkable number of 37,474 peaks were detected and remained after the application of denoising techniques on the basis of relative standard deviation. Following the preprocessing steps, a total of 24,329 peaks were successfully retained. The vacant values were subsequently replenished with one-half of the minimum value. Additionally, the data analysis involved the use of the internal standard normalization method. The dataset, encompassing the peak number, sample name, and normalized peak area, was imported into the SIMCA 16.0.2 software package (Sartorius Stedim Data Analytics AB, Umea, Sweden) to conduct multivariate analysis. The data underwent scaling and logarithmic transformations to mitigate the influence of noise and the substantial variability of the variables. Following the aforementioned transformations, the application of principal component analysis (PCA), an unsupervised analysis technique aimed at reducing data dimensionality, facilitated the visualization of sample distribution and grouping. The threshold for identifying potential outliers in the dataset was determined via a 95% confidence interval in the PCA score plot.

Supervised orthogonal projections to latent structure-discriminate analysis (OPLS-DA) was utilized to facilitate the visualization of group separation and the identification of significantly altered metabolites. Next, a 7-fold cross-validation was conducted to determine the R2 and Q2 values. The R2 metric quantifies the extent to which the variability of a variable can be accounted for by a given model. On the other hand, the Q2 metric assesses the predictive capability of a model in terms of how accurately it can forecast the value of a variable. A permutation analysis was performed 200 times to evaluate the robustness and predictive ability of the OPLS-DA model.

The intercept values for R2 and Q2 were subsequently acquired. The intercept value of Q2 is indicative of the model’s robustness, risk of overfitting, and reliability. A smaller intercept value is desirable, as it signifies better performance in these aspects. Additionally, the variable importance in the projection (VIP) value for the first principal component was obtained through OPLS-DA. The model’s summary provides an overview of the contribution made by each variable. Metabolites exhibiting a VIP value greater than 1 and a p value less than 0.05, as determined by Student’s t test, were classified as statistically significant changes in metabolites. Furthermore, pathway enrichment analysis was conducted via commercial databases such as KEGG (http://www.genome.jp/kegg) and MetaboAnalyst (http://www.metaboanalyst.ca). Receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve analysis was also performed, and the area under the curve (AUC) was calculated to evaluate the diagnostic potential of key metabolites.

sec.title: Confirmation of the pure compounds
sec.p: To test the antagonism of the main compounds synthesized by the MU-10 strain, we selected only the commercially available compounds from the VIP compounds from the metabolomic analysis. Moreover, all of them were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. A dual culture method was adopted to determine the bioactivity of these compounds. For the bioactivity of the solid compounds, 1 g of each compound was dissolved in 100 mL of distilled water. Aliquots of 100, 200, and 300 µL were used for treatment. Similarly, liquid compounds were prepared at a 10% (v/v) dilution, and treatment volumes of 150, 200, and 250 µL were applied. A total of 18 mL of PDA was poured into 90 mm petri plates, then inoculated with 500 µL of a spore suspension (10 × 7 spores/mL) of C. arachidichola. Similarly, the effects of these compounds on the morphology of C. arachidichola were observed via SEM, as mentioned in the above section.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: One-way ANOVA was used to analyze the data, and significance was determined via Tukey’s HSD test with a threshold of p < 0.05. The statistics were performed via IBM SPSS version 25.0. A statistically significant difference was not observed between the means with the same letters. To denote the statistical significance of the pairwise comparisons, the superscripts a, b, and c are used. All the analyses were based on three replications of the treatments.

sec.title: Molecular, morphological, physiological, and biochemical identification of the MU-10 strain
sec.p: Analysis of the 16S rDNA sequence and gyrA gene classified the isolated strain MU-10 under the B. amyloliquefaciens family (Fig. 1A). SEM revealed that strain MU-10 exhibited a typical rod shape, resembling those of other strains of B. amyloliquefaciens (Fig. 1B).

caption.p: A Phylogenetic tree inferred from a combined alignment of the 16S rRNA gene and gyrA sequences. The Bayesian consensus tree was built via 2 million generations, and the first 25% of the samples were excluded as burn-in. The support for each branch was measured by its posterior probability value. Posterior probability values appear on the branches. The scale bar shows 0.03 expected changes per site. B SEM micrograph of strain MU-10

sec.p: The biochemical and physiological characteristics of the isolate were consistent with those of B. amyloliquefaciens. The strain was positive for gelatin liquefaction, catalase enzyme activity, nitrate reduction, starch hydrolysis, citrate utilization, the methyl red test, oxidase activity, and Gram staining. The isolate was able to tolerate salt concentrations up to 10% and exhibited growth over a wide temperature range of 5–45 °C. In contrast, the strain was negative for hydrogen sulfide production and the Voges–Proskauer (VP) reaction. Collectively, these biochemical and physiological properties support the identification of the isolate as B. amyloliquefaciens (Table S1).

sec.title: In vitro bioassays of the effects of strain MU-10 against C. arachidicola
sec.p: In vitro experiments revealed that strain MU-10 exhibited potent antifungal activity against C. arachidicola. On the basis of the results shown in Fig. 2A, the OP fermentation broth from strain MU-10 had a 76 mm inhibition zone against C. arachidicola, whereas it was 68.42 mm for NOP and 70.39 mm for CF-CK. An illustration of the negative control with other treatments is shown in Fig. 2B. In the negative control, C. arachidicola grew strongly, while it was strongly inhibited in the treatment groups.

caption.p: A In vitro bioassay of strain MU-10 against C. arachidicola (Ca). CF; commercial fertilizer, CK; positive control, Ca (CK) negative control, OP; optimized broth, NOP; nonoptimized broth. All the values are the means of three replicates. The superscripts a, b, and c indicate statistical significance. There is no significant difference among the same letters, and significance was determined via Tukey’s HSD test with a threshold of p < 0.05. B An illustration of the negative control in in vitro experiments with other treatments. Ca = C. arachidicola. C SEM micrograph of C. arachidicola showing the effect of MU-10 on Ca. In the CK, there was no Ca treatment. In the figure, S = Spores, and H = Hyphae, white circles indicate the affected part of the mycelium of Ca. D An illustration of the negative control of the greenhouse experiments used to evaluate peanut leaf spot disease

sec.title: Action of the Mu-10 strain against C. arachidicola mycelia and spores
sec.p: SEM revealed significant changes in the morphology of C. arachidicola. The hyphae appeared broken, ruptured, and roughened. Additionally, the abundance of spores was reduced due to the action of MU-10 (Fig. 2C). On the other hand, within the Ck group, the mycelium of C. arachidicola exhibited a smooth and regular shape, accompanied by an abundant number of spores (Fig. 2C).

sec.title: Disease control effects in greenhouse
sec.p: Experiments conducted in a greenhouse environment revealed that strain MU-10 effectively managed peanut leaf spot disease. On the basis of these findings, MU-10. OP demonstrated a disease control efficiency of 93.33%, which was the same as that of the CF control group. However, MU-10 NOP exhibited a slightly lower control efficiency of 89.33%, as depicted in Table 1. Similarly, the disease incidence was 93.34% in the negative control group, whereas it decreased to 6.67% with MU-10. OP and CF treatment. In contrast, the disease incidence with MU-10. The NOP treatment resulted in a value of 10.67%, as indicated in Table 1. Figure 2D shows a comparison between the negative control and the treated groups. The results revealed that an abundance of leaf spot occurred on peanuts, whereas no disease was detected in peanuts that were treated with MU-10. OP, MU-10NOP, and CF.

caption.p: Green house assay, disease incidence, and disease control effects of the MU-10 strain

table-wrap-foot.p: Means sharing similar letter in a column are statistically non-significant (P < 0.05). Note; - indicating, no disease incidence or no disease control. All the values are the mean of three replicates

sec.title: Quality control of the samples
sec.p: We repeatedly injected pooled QC samples during LC‒MS analysis to ensure analytical robustness. QC total ion chromatograms revealed nearly identical peak intensities and retention times (Figure S1A–B), indicating minimal instrumental drift. The internal standard exhibited a low relative standard deviation (RSD) of < 0.3 across the detected features, indicating the stable performance of LC‒MS (Table S2). Similarly, strong Pearson correlations among the QC samples (Figure S1C) demonstrated high reproducibility. Multivariate control charts confirmed that every QC injection remained within acceptable statistical bounds (Figure S1D). These findings confirm that the metabolomics dataset is stable and suitable for further biological interpretation.

sec.title: Multivariate analysis of metabolites
sec.p: The score plot generated by orthogonal projection to latent structures (OPLS) distinguishes between profiles of optimized fermentation broth and nonoptimized fermentation broth fermented controls. In Fig. 3A, t½1 P denotes the predicted principal component score of the first component. t½1° represents the orthogonal principal component acquired. The control group is represented by blue squares, and the optimized fermentation broth group is represented by red dots, making the two groups easy to distinguish. elling’s t-square test revealed that every sample in both groups was above the 95% confidence interval. Permutation tests (n = 200) validated the OPLS-DA model and determined the random model’s R² and Q² values.

caption.p: A OPLS score plot model of OP vs. NOP (R2X = 0.833). B Permutation test results of the OPLS-DA model of OP vs. NOP. C Donut plot classification chart for all the metabolites. D Volcano plot visually displaying the DEMs between the OP and NOP groups. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, and ***P < 0.001. P values were calculated via two-sample t tests

sec.p: The permutation test results for the OPLS-DA model are shown in Fig. 3B. According to the figure, the original model R2Y is 0.998, which is close to 1. This suggests that the established model aligns with the sample data. The Q2 value is 0.995 and is also very close to 1. This indicates that including more samples will likely yield a similar distribution. The permuted R2 values on the left of the intercept are lower than the original R2 value on the right. Similarly, all the Q2 values on the permuted data, set to the left, are lower than the Q2 value on the right. These findings suggest that the original model is robust and does not overfit.

sec.title: Univariate analysis and differentially abundant metabolites
sec.p: On the basis of their chemical classification and attribution information, the identified metabolites are classified and quantified. To illustrate these results, a categorical and tri-level proportional chart represents the secondary classification of various metabolites in the HMDB. Notably, the findings revealed that the abundance of organic acids and their derivatives peaked, accounting for 32% of all metabolites (Fig. 3C).

sec.title: Differentially expressed metabolites
sec.p: A thorough analysis of the OP and NOP of strain MU-10 revealed 58 DEMs in positive ion mode and 25 DEMs in negative ion mode (Table S3). Volcano plot analysis of metabolites from OP and NOP fermentation revealed several differentially expressed metabolites (DEMs) with strong statistical significance and high variable importance in projection (VIP) scores (> 1.0). The most significant DEMs were downregulated in OP compared with NOP, indicating reduced accumulation under nutrient-optimized conditions. These metabolites included syringic acid, maltotriose, forsythiaside, LysoPE (18:0), pyrrole-2-carboxylic acid, hydroxysanthin-5,6-epoxide, and other related carbohydrate-, lipid-, and phenolic-derived compounds. In contrast, a smaller subset of metabolites, specifically vanillylamine and 3-O-acetylpiperazinedione, were significantly upregulated in OP, resulting in high VIP values and suggesting a key role in metabolic differentiation between the two fermentation media.

These findings suggest that nutrient optimization induces targeted metabolic reprogramming, characterized by the suppression of several secondary and stress-associated metabolites and the selective enhancement of nitrogen-containing and aromatic compounds rather than broad metabolome-wide changes (Fig. 3D). These substances, including vanillylamine and 3-O-acetylated diterpenoids, were consistently enriched in the OP group, whereas a number of metabolites linked to antibiotics, lipids, and carbohydrates were decreased. These patterns are supported by box plot visualization (Figure S2A-L), which also shows decreased within-group variability under ideal circumstances, suggesting stricter metabolic regulation.

sec.title: Heatmap clustering and correlation analysis
sec.p: Heatmap clustering via euclidean distance clearly distinguished OP samples from NOP samples. This analysis revealed distinct clusters of metabolites whose levels increased or decreased together (Figure S3A). Correlation analysis confirmed that most DEMs changed in a positively coordinated manner, suggesting that optimization affected interconnected metabolic modules, not isolated reactions (Figure S3B).

sec.title: KEGG pathway annotation and enrichment
sec.p: All the detected metabolites were annotated via the KEGG database. Of the 564 identified small molecules, 53 were mapped to KEGG pathways, resulting in 27 unique pathways after removal of redundancy (Supplementary Table S4). KEGG classification analysis revealed that the differentially abundant metabolites were involved mainly in membrane transport, amino acid metabolism, carbohydrate metabolism, lipid metabolism, biosynthesis, and signal transduction (Fig. 4A). Pathways related to membrane transport, particularly ABC transporters (16.67%) and the phosphotransferase system (PTS) (11.11%), were the most abundant. Furthermore, the metabolic pathways of cysteine and methionine metabolism, amino sugar and nucleotide sugar metabolism, glycerophospholipid metabolism, and the biosynthesis of nucleotide sugars were notably prevalent.

caption.p: A KEGG classification of functional metabolites. B Differentially abundant metabolites in the KEGG metabolic pathway Rich factor. C Metabolic pathway analysis. D Regulatory network analysis of differentially abundant metabolites

sec.p: KEGG enrichment analysis subsequently pinpointed the pathways most significantly impacted by nutrient optimization (Fig. 4B). ABC transporters demonstrated the greatest enrichment, followed by the biosynthesis of nucleotide sugars; conversely, PTS, the two-component system, glycerophospholipid metabolism, and cysteine and methionine metabolism were moderately enriched. These findings collectively suggest that nutrient optimization primarily influences pathways linked to substrate transport, carbohydrate utilization, amino acid metabolism, and membrane-related processes.

sec.title: Metabolic pathway topology and network analysis
sec.p: Integrated pathway topology analysis pinpointed the synthesis and degradation of ketone bodies as a central hub pathway affected by optimization, whereas pathways such as amino sugar and nucleotide sugar metabolism presented a lesser overall impact (Fig. 4C). Network analysis of the most significant KEGG pathways revealed extensive interactions among metabolites, enzymes, and reactions, highlighting the systemic character of the metabolic response. Specifically, acetoacetic acid functions as a central node, linking numerous reactions within the ketone body pathway, which suggests its potential role in mediating the metabolic and bioactive effects of fermentation optimization (Fig. 4D).

sec.title: ROC analysis of metabolites
sec.p: Receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves were used to define the top potential metabolite biomarkers for the metabolites (Figure S4 A- J). In this case, the Keys metabolites pyrrole-2-carboxylic acid and syringic acid had AUC and ci values of 1 (Figure S4C, and S4J). However, vanillylamine had a ci value of 0.895 and an AUC value of 0.97 (Figure S4K). These findings confirm the validity of the model and support the results of the metabolomic analysis.

sec.title: Bioactivity confirmation of pure compounds
sec.p: The results showed that pyrrole-2-carboxylic acid had potent antifungal activity. According to the results, Pyrrole-2-carboxylic acid exhibited an inhibition rate of 85% at a dosage of 300 µL. Even the 200 µL and 100 µL dosages also resulted in strong antifungal activity. Vanillylamine, with 65%, and syringic acid, with 53%, showed slightly less activity. Moreover, erythrono-1,4-lactone did not show any antifungal activity.

Surprisingly, vanillylamine exhibited a more potent inhibition rate of 67% at a dosage of 200 µL, as shown in Fig. 5. As shown in Fig. 6, these compounds exhibited potent antifungal activity. The results confirmed that these compounds are responsible for bioactivity. Similarly, these compounds deformed the morphology of the pathogen. The results showed that pyrrole-2-carboxylic acid severely affected the morphology of mycelia and even affected spores. Similarly, other compounds, vanillylamine and syringic acid, also affected the morphology of mycelia and spores, as shown in Fig. 6B.

caption.p: Bioactive confirmatory test of VIP compounds against C. arachidicola by vanillylamine (Va), syringic acid (SA), and pyrrole-2-carboxylic acid. All the values are the means of three replicates. The superscripts a, b, and c indicate statistical significance. There is no significant difference among the same letters, and significance was determined via Tukey’s HSD test with a threshold of p < 0.05

A Antifungal results of the bioactive confirmatory test of VIP compounds against C. arachidicola by vanillylamine (Va), syringic acid (SA), and pyrrole-2-carboxylic acid (PA). In Ck, there was no treatment involving only C. arachidicola. B SEM micrograph of C. arachidicola, showing the effects of vanillylamine (Va), syringic acid (SA), and pyrrole-2-carboxylic acid (PA). In Ck, there was no treatment involving only C. arachidicola

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The strain MU-10 was isolated and identified as B. amyloliquefaciens. These biochemical and physiological traits are characteristic of B. amyloliquefaciens and align well with previously reported descriptions of the species [34]. The observed enzymatic activities and broad temperature and salt tolerance further support its ecological adaptability and taxonomic identification. The results demonstrated that strain MU-10 exhibited potent antagonistic activity against C. arachidicola to manage peanut leaf spot disease. The current investigation aligns with previous reports describing the broad-spectrum efficacy of Bacillus species against phytopathogens [36, 37]. Furthermore, in vitro assays demonstrated that OP broth from strain MU-10 significantly inhibited the growth of C. arachidicola. Notably, optimization of fermentation medium nutrients resulted in a marked increase in antifungal activity [38]. Similarly, greenhouse experiments revealed that the MU-10 strain suppressed peanut leaf spot disease occurrence. This study indicates its potential as a potent and reliable biological control agent. According to recent studies, in greenhouse settings, the strain B. amyloliquefaciens RaSh1 successfully suppressed Alternaria alternata in Capsicum annuum [39]. Similarly, strain BA-4 was effective against Fusarium in apple plants [40].

SEM analysis revealed that the ultrastructure of the hyphae of the pathogen was strongly impacted by strain MU-10, and the abundance of spores even decreased. In a recent study, the B. amyloliquefaciens F9 strain exhibited the same mechanism of action against Xanthomonas citri [41]. In the present study, the MU-10 strain produced distinct bioactive secondary metabolites. UHPLC/MS-MS analysis revealed distinct metabolites in both the OP and NOP fermentation broths of strain MU-10, facilitating comparative metabolic profiling to identify differences between these conditions.

These metabolites encompass a diverse array of secondary metabolite classes. On the basis of our findings, the donut plot demonstrated that the prevalence of organic acids and their derivatives reached its peak, constituting 32% of the overall metabolites. During carbohydrate metabolism, a reduction in sugar leads to the production of organic acids through fermentation.

This, in turn, has a significant effect on the acidity of the culture environment, thereby influencing the overall suitability and livability of the microbial population within this environment [42]. The antifungal properties of these organic acids have been reported by Jin et al. (2021) [43]. Several DEMs and metabolites with high VIP scores, such as 3-O-acetylepisamarcandin, vanillylamine, neokestose, maltotetraose, syringic acid, pyrrole-2-carboxylic acid, hydroxysintaxanthin 5,6-epoxide, lysophospholipids (LysoPC 18:1, LysoPE 15:0), and erythrono-1,4-lactone, were identified through metabolomic analysis. Because of increased microbial metabolic activity and better nutrient availability, the majority of these metabolites accumulated at higher levels in the optimized medium. A number of substances, including 3-O-acetylepisamarcandin, vanillylamine, and syringic acid, are plant-associated molecules that are likely obtained from semolina flour, beef extract, or other medium components and undergo additional modification via bacterial enzymatic activity.

Carbohydrate metabolites such as neokestose, maltotetraose, and erythrono-1,4-lactone indicate the active processing of medium sugars, whereas lysophospholipids reflect membrane remodeling under nutrient-rich conditions.

The composition of the medium directly influences the metabolic profile of the bacteria, as described by Kim et al., 2017 [44]. 3-O-Acetylepisamarcandin is classified as a coumarin and derivative and is an organic compound. Coumarins, which are natural heterocycles, significantly contribute to the structural framework of diverse biologically active compounds, as elucidated by El-Sawy et al. in their recent publication (2021) [45]. Vanillylamine belongs to the class of organic compounds known as amines, which are crucial chemical entities utilized extensively in the chemical industry for the synthesis of diverse pharmaceuticals, agrochemicals, cleaning agents, personal care products, and polymers [46].

Other important compounds have been shown to downregulate the expression of 8 secondary metabolites, namely, 2-{[3,4-dihydroxy-2,5-bis(hydroxymethyl)oxolan-2-yl]oxy}-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxane-3,4,5-triol (neokestose). Neokestose compounds are classified as nucleosides. Nucleoside antibiotics include a wide array of naturally occurring compounds that carry out various biological functions [47, 48]. The findings of Chen et al. (2016) [49] show that these nucleoside antibiotics have significant efficacy in preventing pathogenic infections in both mammals and plants. 2,3-Dihydro-4-(4-methoxyphenyl)-1 H-phenalene-1,2,3-triol belongs to the class of organic compounds referred to as phenylnaphthalenes. A 2017 study by Gotor-Vila et al. [50] revealed that organic compounds derived from B. amyloliquefaciens CPA-8 exhibited notable antifungal effects on Monilinia laxa, M. fructicola, and Botrytis cinerea.

These three pathogens are commonly associated with postharvest fruit deterioration in sweet cherry fruit. Another important DEM whose expression is downregulated is forsythiasides, and according to Yang et al. (2022) [51], forsythiasides are a subclass of phenylethanol glycosides. Notably, phenylethanol glycoside compounds exhibit pronounced biological activity and significant pharmacological attributes, as described by Xue et al. (2017) [52]. Similarly, in downregulated DEMs, maltotetraose was another compound that belongs to the class of organic compounds known as oligosaccharides. Ruilian et al., 2021 [53] demonstrated the antifungal activity of an oligosaccharide analog. The mechanism of action involves the perturbation of cell wall organization through the specific targeting of PHR transglucosidases.

Among the DEMs, the next metabolite was syringic acid, which is a natural phenolic compound. Syringic acid has potential health benefits, such as antioxidant, antimicrobe, anti-inflammatory, anticancer, and antidiabetes properties [54]. Erythrono-1,4-lactone is classified as a gamma butyrolactone, which is a class of organic compounds. γ-Butyrolactone, a cyclic ester consisting of a five-membered ring, is considered a structurally advantageous component found in a wide range of naturally occurring substances and bioactive compounds. A substantial focus has been placed on the development of synthetic methodologies for the production of ‘butyrolactones’ because of their broad range of biological and pharmacological properties [55].

LysoPC(18:1(9Z) and LysoPE(15:0/0:0) are both organic compounds known as 1-acyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholines. Plueckthun and Dennis 1982 [56] described 1-acyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholines as intermediates in lipid metabolism. Another important DEM is astaxanthin epoxide. Maoka et al., 2020 [57] demonstrated that astaxanthin forms hydroxyl radicals and superoxide anion radicals after reacting with both radicals. 5,9-Epidioxy-3-hydroxyergost-7-en-6-ones are classified as ergostane steroids, which are organic compounds characterized by a molecular structure consisting of heteropolycyclic compounds. It is widely recognized that heterocycles are useful organic molecules because of their high bioactivity, which can be applied in drug development, agriculture, and other areas [58] (Li et al., 2021). Box plot analysis revealed that the OP group presented higher concentrations of 3-O-acetylepisamarcandin and vanillylamine than did the other metabolite groups. These findings indicated that the DEMs whose expression was upregulated were present at higher concentrations in the OP group than those whose expression was downregulated in the NOP group.

According to a study conducted by Masutin et al. in 2022 [59], a majority of the metabolites were significantly upregulated. These metabolites can be considered the primary focus of interest in this study. According to the findings of Wieder et al. (2021) [60], in the domain of untargeted metabolomics, many metabolites, ranging from hundreds to thousands, are frequently subjected to profiling. Consequently, it becomes imperative to employ multiple testing correction techniques to ensure the validity and reliability of the obtained results. Heatmap analysis of metabolites among OP and NOP revealed the abundance of key metabolites, which are DEMs. Therefore, these analyses support that key metabolites shift during the process between these two comparative groups. Classification and comparative analysis of the metabolites of the experimental groups are facilitated through the utilization of this approach, as elucidated by Nathalie et al. (2019) [61]. Correlation analysis indicated that metabolite regulation during biological state changes was stable. System–metabolite correlations typically show metabolite synergy [62]. The KEGG pathway annotation revealed a total of 27 distinct categories.

Pathway analysis is an essential component in the elucidation of complex molecular information, particularly in high-dimensional datasets. Pathways are groups of molecules that work together to perform a specific biological task. This method is used to find connections between pathways and a very important phenotype [63, 64]. KEGG pathway analysis revealed ABC transporters as the most prominent pathway. The high representation of these metabolites in the optimized medium suggests that B. amyloliquefaciens actively modulates the uptake of sugars, amino acids, and peptides, as well as the export of metabolites, corresponding to the greater abundance of medium-derived and transformed metabolites. These transporters enable bacteria to absorb nutrients and release toxins and antimicrobials, making them potent [65, 66].

A topological study of the KEGG pathway revealed that the metabolic pathway is influenced primarily by the processes of ketone body synthesis and degradation. The metabolic process responsible for the production of ketone bodies is commonly referred to as ketogenesis, whereas the catabolic pathway involved in the breakdown of ketone bodies is known as ketolysis. Acetyl-coenzyme A (acetyl-CoA), a crucial metabolite generated through the β-oxidation of fatty acids within the mitochondria, serves as a precursor for the biosynthesis of ketones. The catabolism of fatty acids, which is dependent on adenosine triphosphate (ATP), is characterized by the breakdown of two carbon fragments in a sequential manner, ultimately leading to the production of acetyl-CoA [67].

Network analysis of KEGG pathways further revealed that several hub metabolites (red nodes) are highly connected to multiple enzymes and reactions. These hubs, including sugars, acetyl-CoA derivatives, and medium-transformed compounds, are central to metabolic flux, linking pathways such as carbohydrate metabolism, energy metabolism, and bioactive compound synthesis. The network confirmed that optimized nutrient conditions enhance substrate processing, enzymatic activity, and metabolite transport, supporting the accumulation of key DEMs and VIP score compounds with antifungal activity. The top 10 pathways and differentially abundant metabolites and enzymes interact in a complicated fashion. Tiers of the top 10 pathways correlated with metabolite changes [68] (Feng et al.,2022).

The ROC curves demonstrated that the majority of the metabolites presented an area under the curve (AUC) value of 1, indicating high diagnostic accuracy. Erythrono-1,4-lactone, 3-O-Acetyle pisamarcandin, and vanillylamine, on the other hand, had AUC values less than 1, which means that they are less useful for diagnostic purposes. The area under the curve (AUC) is a measure of diagnostic performance. A higher AUC, closer to 1, indicates a more favorable diagnostic effect. The accuracy of a given AUC value decreases when it falls within the range of 0.5–0.7. Conversely, when the AUC value falls within the range of 0.7–0.9, it exhibits a level of accuracy that can be considered certain. Furthermore, an AUC value exceeding 0.9 indicates a higher degree of accuracy. When the area under the curve (AUC) is equal to 0.5, the diagnostic modality is completely ineffective and has no diagnostic utility [69] (Nahm 2022). Confirmatory tests revealed that pyrrole-2-carboxylic acid, vanillylamine, and syringic acid exhibited antifungal activity against C. arachidicola. As mentioned above, these compounds are reported to be antimicrobial compounds. For the confirmatory test of the identified compounds, commercial compounds were previously reported by Zhang et al. (2023) [70].

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The B. amyloliquefaciens MU-10 strain shows strong promise as an effective and environmentally friendly method for controlling peanut early leaf spot. This strain effectively inhibits fungal growth in laboratory tests and greenhouse experiments, thereby reducing plant sickness. MU-10 damages the strands of the disease-causing fungus, confirming that it fights the pathogen directly. By analyzing the chemicals produced, we found that changing the growth medium altered the beneficial substances produced by MU-10, and optimized conditions increased the production of important antifungal compounds. The discovery of key chemicals, especially 3-O-acetylisopimaric acid, vanillylamine, and syringic acid, highlights their role in preventing disease, and further tests confirm their importance. Overall, this work helps us better understand how these chemicals function and highlights the commercial potential of MU-10’s natural products for controlling peanut early leaf spot in an environmentally safe manner.
